sectors.name: Semiconductors
sectors.description: packaged chip ready for system assembly

tiers.fn: Produce the ultra-high-purity silicon, specialty gases, photoresists, CMP slurries, and process chemicals that every subsequent fab step consumes

companies.name: Shin-Etsu Chemical
companies.notes: Dominates two irreplaceable chip-making inputs at once — #1 in silicon wafers AND in EUV photoresist chemicals. Losing either supply would halt production at every major global chip factory.

companies.name: AGC Inc.
companies.notes: Near-monopoly on the ultra-pure quartz glass used as EUV photomask substrates — a niche so narrow that almost no alternative supplier exists at commercial scale.

tiers.fn: Slice, grind, etch, and polish silicon boules to produce the atomically flat, contamination-free substrates on which all CMOS devices are built

companies.name: Sumco Corporation
companies.notes: One of only two companies that can supply 300mm silicon wafers at foundry scale — the global market is essentially a duopoly and Sumco holds the #2 position in it.

tiers.fn: Grow gallium arsenide, indium phosphide, gallium nitride, and silicon carbide crystals and epitaxial layers for photonic, RF, and power devices that silicon cannot serve

companies.name: 5N Plus Inc.
companies.notes: Supplies ultra-pure compound semiconductor materials — gallium arsenide, indium phosphide — that epitaxial wafer growers use to produce III-V substrates. One of very few producers outside China at commercial semiconductor-grade purity.

companies.name: STMicroelectronics
companies.notes: SiC power MOSFET leader with deep fab ownership — vertically integrating from SiC substrate through finished power device. A single EV contains 10x more SiC content than a conventional car, making automotive electrification the primary revenue driver.

tiers.fn: Provide the electronic design automation software and licensable chip IP that allows engineers to design billion-transistor chips without understanding every process detail

companies.name: Arm Holdings
companies.notes: Licenses the CPU blueprints that 99% of smartphones and a fast-growing share of datacenters are built on — every chip designer pays ARM a royalty on every chip shipped, making it a royalty on global compute growth without manufacturing a single transistor.

tiers.fn: Design the logic, memory, and analog circuits that define a chip’s function, then optimize for performance, power, and area (PPA) at a target process node — the highest-value-add step in the semiconductor industry

companies.name: NVIDIA Corporation
companies.notes: Runs roughly 80% of AI model training worldwide. Its real moat is CUDA — 15+ years of developer software the whole industry is built on, making switching to AMD or custom chips nearly impossible.

companies.name: Marvell Technology
companies.notes: The only chip designer profiting whether AI clusters use NVIDIA GPUs or custom silicon — it designs the custom AI chips Amazon and Google build to reduce NVIDIA dependence, and the networking chips that connect them.

companies.name: Advanced Micro Devices
companies.notes: NVIDIA's closest GPU rival, with MI300X/MI400 gaining AI inference share at major hyperscalers — also the dominant x86 server CPU against Intel. The #2 AI accelerator company, with a faster design cadence than Intel and a credible path to 20%+ AI GPU market share.

companies.name: Broadcom
companies.notes: Designs the custom AI ASICs powering Google's TPU and Meta's MTIA — the dominant non-NVIDIA supplier of AI training silicon. Also controls the datacenter Ethernet switching chip market (Tomahawk series) that Arista's switches run on, two independent AI infrastructure revenue streams.

companies.name: Apple Inc.
companies.notes: The world's largest fabless chip designer by revenue — its A-series (iPhone) and M-series (Mac) chips consistently beat Intel and Qualcomm on performance-per-watt, setting the benchmark for what custom silicon achieves when chip design is the core competitive differentiator.

companies.name: Intel Corporation
companies.notes: x86 CPU market leader fighting on two fronts: defending its data center CPU franchise against AMD while spending $100B+ to build a competitive US foundry and re-enter AI accelerators with Gaudi. The highest-stakes turnaround in semiconductors — and strategically critical to US chip independence.

companies.name: Texas Instruments
companies.notes: Analog and embedded processing leader with 80,000+ product SKUs — its chips manage power delivery, sensing, and signal conditioning in virtually every industrial, automotive, and consumer device. Not an AI capex play, but an essential building block of every AI-adjacent end market.

companies.name: NXP Semiconductors
companies.notes: Dominates automotive microcontrollers and in-vehicle networking chips — an average car shipped today contains 5–7 NXP MCUs. Also leads in secure NFC chips used in contactless payments and access control, a defensive revenue stream alongside automotive.

companies.name: D-Wave Quantum
companies.notes: The only publicly traded quantum annealer — solves discrete optimization problems (routing, scheduling, logistics) using a different architecture than gate-based quantum computers. Commercially deployed with enterprise customers; not a general-purpose computer and not directly competing with IBM or Google Quantum.

companies.name: Rigetti Computing
companies.notes: Gate-based quantum computing startup building full-stack hardware and cloud access for quantum algorithms — competing against IBM Quantum and Google Quantai for developer mindshare. Pre-revenue at scale; the thesis is quantum provides exponential speedup for specific problem classes.

tiers.fn: Design the retimers, SerDes PHYs, and PCIe/CXL connectivity chips that move data between accelerators, between servers, and across the rack at 112G/224G per lane — the electrical signaling layer that lets compute and memory talk

companies.name: Astera Labs
companies.notes: Makes the signal-booster (retimer) chips that keep AI servers connected at full speed as rack counts scale — a bottleneck that only appeared when GPU clusters grew to thousands of cards, creating a niche with no entrenched incumbent.

companies.name: Credo Technology
companies.notes: Solves the physics of moving data at 224 billion bits per second between chips on a board — its active-electrical-cable (AEC) approach is more power-efficient than optical for short distances inside a server, and that advantage compounds with every GPU generation.

tiers.fn: Design and fabricate the high-bandwidth and high-density memory — HBM stacks, DDR/LPDDR DRAM, and NAND flash — that feeds AI accelerators and stores model weights, datasets, and KV cache. The layer compute is useless without.

companies.name: Micron Technology
companies.notes: The only US-based maker of High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) — the specialized memory stacked directly on AI chips. Without HBM, a GPU can't move data fast enough to stay productive during training. One of three HBM suppliers worldwide alongside SK Hynix and Samsung.

tiers.fn: Project photomask circuit patterns onto photoresist-coated wafers at nanometer precision using UV or EUV light — the single most critical and concentrated manufacturing equipment tier

companies.name: ASML Holding
companies.notes: The only company that builds the machines used to print the world's most advanced chips. Without ASML's EUV scanners, TSMC and Samsung cannot manufacture chips at 7nm or below — no other company can do this.

tiers.fn: Deposit thin-film layers atom-by-atom and etch features with sub-angstrom control to build transistors, interconnects, and dielectrics across 100+ process steps
Measure every critical dimension and detect every printable defect in real time — the feedback loop that enables yield learning and process control
Execute 500–1,500 process steps to transform a blank wafer into a wafer full of functional die, integrating all upstream tiers into a manufacturable, yielding process

companies.name: TSMC
companies.notes: Manufactures every leading-edge AI chip on earth — NVIDIA, Apple, and AMD all depend on TSMC, and no other company can produce 3nm chips at commercial scale. It also controls CoWoS, the advanced chip-stacking process inside every NVIDIA H100 and H200.

companies.name: GlobalFoundries
companies.notes: Focuses on RF and automotive chips at older process nodes with US domestic fabs — fills a supply chain security gap for customers who cannot use overseas foundries for sensitive or government applications.

tiers.fn: Integrate multiple die onto a silicon interposer or via 3D hybrid bonding — CoWoS, SoIC, InFO — and join HBM stacks beside the GPU. The fastest-changing and most capacity-constrained step in the AI hardware chain.

companies.name: Amkor Technology
companies.notes: One of only three or four companies globally capable of assembling the advanced multi-chip packages NVIDIA's H100 and H200 require — fills overflow capacity when TSMC's own CoWoS lines are fully booked.

tiers.fn: Dice wafers, wire-bond or flip-chip die into conventional packages, and assemble the system-in-package at volume — the high-scale outsourced assembly and test tier beneath advanced packaging.
Electrically verify every die and packaged device; stress-screen for latent defects; characterize performance bins for yield optimization and product segmentation

companies.name: Aehr Test Systems
companies.notes: Has a near-monopoly on wafer-level burn-in testing for silicon carbide (SiC) chips — SiC is the key semiconductor in EV powertrains and industrial inverters, making Aehr the quality gatekeeper for a fast-growing supply chain.

tam.ai_driver: AI accelerator demand (GPU, custom ASICs, inference chips) is the primary growth engine — data center semiconductors alone forecast to reach $843B by 2030, nearly half of total market revenue.

sectors.name: Electronic Components
sectors.description: populated PCB ready for system assembly

tiers.fn: Synthesize and refine the functional materials that passive components are built from
Produce the insulating base layers and build-up films that PCBs and IC substrates are constructed from
Stack, fire, and terminate ceramic multilayer chip capacitors — the most ubiquitous passive component on any PCB
Produce high-voltage and high-capacitance capacitors for power electronics, filtering, and energy storage
Wind, press, or print inductive components for power conversion, filtering, and signal isolation
Etch, drill, plate, and laminate copper circuit layers onto insulating substrates to create printed circuit boards
Build the fine-pitch interposer layers that bridge IC die to PCB — the most technically demanding segment of the package supply chain
Form, plate, and assemble the mechanical and electrical interfaces that link boards, modules, cables, and racks
Place and solder all components onto populated PCBs at volume; the final integration step before system-level assembly

tam.ai_driver: AI GPU packaging (CoWoS 2.5D/3D stacking for H100/B100 series and HBM integration) is the fastest-growing segment — CoWoS wafer demand is forecast to triple from 370K wafers in 2024 to over 1M by 2026.

tiers.fn: Delineate ore bodies and estimate recoverable metal tonnage to justify mine capital commitment
Break, load, and haul rock from the ore body to the processing plant

companies.name: Freeport-McMoRan
companies.notes: The world's largest publicly traded copper miner — copper is the essential conductor in AI datacenter power wiring, EV motors, and grid infrastructure, making FCX a structural beneficiary of the electrification and AI compute build-out without technology risk.

tiers.fn: Liberate and concentrate target minerals from gangue rock to produce a saleable or smeltable concentrate
Reduce metal from concentrate using heat and flux to produce crude metal or matte for further refining
Remove impurities from crude metal to produce refined metal at LME-delivery grade (99.99%+)

companies.name: The Elmet Group Co.
companies.notes: The only U.S.-owned vertically integrated tungsten and molybdenum producer — processes critical refractory metals from powder to finished defense and semiconductor components. China controls ~80% of global tungsten supply, making ELMT the sole domestic alternative as U.S. decoupling policy creates a structural sourcing pull. IPO April 2026; Q1 2026 backlog hit $113M (+52% YoY).

tiers.tier: Rare Earth Separation & Critical Mineral Refining
tiers.fn: Separate individual rare earth elements from mixed concentrate and refine critical byproduct metals to usable purity

companies.name: MP Materials
companies.notes: The only rare earth miner and processor at commercial scale outside China — its Mountain Pass mine produces the neodymium-praseodymium needed for EV motors, wind turbines, and defense systems. China controls ~90% of global rare earth processing, making MP strategically critical.

tiers.fn: Upgrade metallurgical-grade silicon to polysilicon and SiC powder grades required for semiconductor and power-device manufacturing
Process lithium, nickel, cobalt, and manganese into battery-ready chemical forms suitable for cathode and electrolyte manufacturing

tam.source: IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2025 / Grand View Research 2024
tam.ai_driver: AI data center buildout is driving surging demand for copper (512Kt additional by 2030 for data centers alone), silicon wafers, and gallium/germanium — China's 2024 export restrictions on the latter two have introduced acute supply-chain risk.
tam.ai_sub_label: Rare earth elements market 2024 (Grand View Research)

sectors.name: Photonics & Optical
sectors.description: optical transceiver enabling AI cluster interconnect

tiers.fn: Grow the compound semiconductor wafers that laser diodes, VCSELs, photodetectors, and GaN power devices are fabricated on

companies.name: AXT Inc.
companies.notes: One of the few US suppliers of InP wafers — the substrate laser diodes are built on — with record backlog as NVIDIA-linked transceiver demand outpaces supply. Domestic feedstock sourcing reduces China exposure.

companies.name: Sivers Semiconductors
companies.notes: European foundry that bridges the gap between raw III-V substrate and finished photonic or 5G chips — serves customers who need compound semiconductor device fabrication but lack their own wafer fab.

companies.name: IQE plc
companies.notes: The world's only pure-play compound semiconductor epiwafer foundry — grows the GaAs, InP, and GaN epitaxial layers that chip designers cannot produce in-house. As laser chip demand for AI datacenters scales, IQE's wafer growth capacity becomes the binding supply constraint one tier above.

tiers.fn: Pattern, implant, metallize, and cleave epitaxial wafers to produce individual photonic chips — laser diodes, EMLs, VCSELs, photodetectors — at wafer scale

companies.name: Coherent Corp
companies.notes: America's largest photonic chip manufacturer and NVIDIA's anchor transceiver supplier — its scale and 3.2T product roadmap make it the market-share leader (~25%) in datacenter transceivers, with breadth no US rival can match.

companies.name: Lumentum Holdings
companies.notes: Sole high-volume supplier of 200G EML chips for AI datacenters, with capacity pre-allocated to NVIDIA through 2027 — a structural sole-source position that gives it pricing power even Coherent's scale cannot displace.

companies.name: Applied Optoelectronics
companies.notes: High-upside, higher-risk alternative to Coherent and Lumentum — among the first to ship 800G transceivers to hyperscalers but with less proven scale; the thesis is it sustains margins as volume ramps to 1.6T.

companies.name: nLIGHT Inc.
companies.notes: The only publicly traded company spanning from semiconductor laser chips all the way through to complete directed-energy weapons — serves defense and industrial markets rather than the datacenter transceiver race.

companies.name: MACOM Technology Solutions
companies.notes: Designs compound semiconductor chips (GaN, InP, GaAs, SiGe) for high-performance applications silicon cannot serve — RF front-ends for 5G base stations and photonic driver ICs inside AI datacenter transceivers. Positioned at the profitable intersection of defense radar and hyperscale optics.

tiers.fn: Integrate optical functions — waveguides, ring modulators, Mach-Zehnder modulators, Ge photodetectors — onto silicon wafers using CMOS-compatible processes to enable chiplet-level optical I/O

companies.name: POET Technologies
companies.notes: Developing a chip platform that merges silicon photonics with compound semiconductor lasers on a single substrate — if successful, replaces expensive manual laser assembly with cheaper wafer-scale production. Pre-revenue.

companies.name: Lightwave Logic
companies.notes: Developing organic electro-optic materials that could be 10x more power-efficient than today's lithium niobate modulators — entirely pre-revenue, pure R&D stage with no commercial product yet.

tiers.fn: Produce the glass preforms and draw the optical fibers that carry photons between chips, racks, data centers, and continents

companies.name: FOCI Fiber Optic Communications
companies.notes: Taiwanese specialty fiber cable assembler — occupies the niche between raw fiber strand and finished transceiver module, making the cable plant components inside datacenters and telecom facilities.

companies.name: Corning Incorporated
companies.notes: The world's #1 optical fiber manufacturer — supplies the glass fiber connecting servers within datacenters, linking datacenters to each other, and crossing oceans. Also makes the low-loss fiber specifically designed for the higher power levels used in coherent AI networking systems.

companies.name: Prysmian S.p.A.
companies.notes: The world's #2 optical fiber manufacturer and leader in submarine and high-complexity grid cables — its OVD draw capacity rivals Corning's, and its energy cable business means it captures the AI datacenter buildout through both fiber and power infrastructure.

companies.name: Furukawa Electric
companies.notes: Japan's leading fiber maker via its OFS subsidiary, which holds key VAD preform IP — one of three companies globally controlling large-scale optical fiber draw, identified alongside Corning and Prysmian in the supply chain structural moat analysis.

tiers.fn: Filter, route, split, combine, and condition light at the wavelength or polarization level without active gain or modulation

companies.name: Viavi Solutions
companies.notes: Two businesses in one: makes the thin-film filters that split wavelengths inside fiber networks, and sells the test instruments used to diagnose fiber faults — a picks-and-shovels play on both sides of the optical infrastructure buildout.

companies.name: Coherent Corp
companies.notes: Extends its laser chip business into passive optical components — isolators, beam combiners, and WDM filters — sold alongside its active photonic products to the same hyperscaler and telecom customers.

tiers.fn: Co-package laser, photodetector, driver IC, TIA, and DSP into pluggable optical modules that translate electrical signals to optical and back at data-center-relevant speeds

companies.name: Coherent Corp
companies.notes: The volume leader in AI datacenter transceivers, shipping 800G and 1.6T modules into the same GPU clusters its laser chips power — a vertically integrated position from photonic chip to finished pluggable module.

companies.name: Lumentum Holdings
companies.notes: Closest rival to Coherent in 800G datacenter transceivers, with a technology lead in PAM4 for shorter-reach AI cluster links — competing directly for the same hyperscaler purchasing decisions.

companies.name: Applied Optoelectronics
companies.notes: Targets the 400G transceiver refresh cycle at a lower price point than Coherent and Lumentum — not yet competing at 800G, where the market leaders have locked up capacity with hyperscalers.

companies.name: Fabrinet
companies.notes: The contract manufacturer that physically assembles most of the industry's optical modules — Coherent, Lumentum, Ciena, and Cisco all outsource production here, making Fabrinet a royalty on the entire transceiver market's volume growth.

companies.name: ShunSin Technology Holdings
companies.notes: Foxconn's precision optical packaging arm doing FAU fiber alignment in NVIDIA's CPO supply chain — a structural margin step-up from 15% OSAT to 30–35% CPO assembly as co-packaged optics replaces pluggable transceivers in AI clusters.

tiers.fn: Transmit, amplify, switch, and manage terabit-class optical signals over metro and long-haul fiber routes connecting data centers and backbone nodes

companies.name: Ciena Corporation
companies.notes: The only large-cap pure-play on long-haul optical transport systems — its WaveLogic DSP has multi-generation performance leadership in squeezing terabits per fiber strand, targeting the datacenter-to-datacenter routes being rebuilt for AI.

companies.name: Lumentum Holdings
companies.notes: Supplies the ROADM photonic switching modules and tunable lasers that Ciena, Nokia, and other system vendors integrate into their metro and long-haul fiber platforms — a component supplier into the same infrastructure buildout.

companies.name: Coherent Corp
companies.notes: Supplies coherent optical modules to telecom equipment vendors — a complementary revenue stream that extends its customer reach beyond its own branded transceiver business.

tiers.fn: Produce optically-based sensors and ranging systems for robotics, autonomous systems, industrial measurement, and environmental monitoring

companies.name: nLIGHT Inc.
companies.notes: High-power fiber lasers for US defense directed-energy programs — a government-contract revenue stream with different cycle dynamics than the AI/datacenter transceiver market.

companies.name: Viavi Solutions
companies.notes: Uses ordinary fiber cable as a distributed sensor — the cable itself detects temperature and vibration along pipelines, power lines, and borders over hundreds of kilometers, with no additional hardware required.

companies.name: Hamamatsu Photonics
companies.notes: The world's dominant photosensor foundry — makes the InGaAs APD arrays and SiPM detectors that most commercial LiDAR systems are built around, with no credible pure-play alternative for high-sensitivity near-infrared detection.

tiers.fn: Characterize, verify, and screen photonic devices from single-component spectral parameters through wafer-level opto-electrical yield verification and in-field coherent link quality assurance

companies.name: Yokogawa Electric
companies.notes: Makes the industry-standard optical spectrum analyzers used to production-test every datacenter laser and transceiver — its AQ6361 is purpose-built for the AI datacenter component testing ramp, with 20x the speed of its predecessor.

companies.name: Anritsu Corporation
companies.notes: Supplies the coherent network testers that certify BER, FEC, and OSNR on 100ZR through 800ZR pluggable transceivers — the field instrument that a coherent optical link must pass before going live in an AI datacenter backbone.

companies.name: Advantest Corporation
companies.notes: World #2 semiconductor ATE company that co-developed the V93000-Triton wafer-level SiPh/CPO test system with FormFactor — the first production-grade opto-electrical ATE, making Advantest the chokepoint for CPO yield verification. Also in Semiconductors sector.

companies.name: FormFactor Inc.
companies.notes: Wafer probe market leader that acquired Keystone Photonics (Dec 2025) and built OptoVue Pro 9-axis optical alignment — the probe hardware half of the V93000-Triton CPO test cell. No SiPh wafer ships at volume without passing through a FormFactor probe.

tam.ai_driver: Hyperscaler AI cluster interconnects (400G/800G optical transceivers for GPU-to-GPU rack-scale networking) are the dominant growth driver — silicon photonics enables co-packaged optics eliminating pluggable modules at the scale required by 100,000+ GPU clusters.

sectors.name: Space & Communications
sectors.description: satellite connectivity enabling AI edge inference and global broadband

tiers.fn: Deliver payloads to orbit via expendable or reusable rockets; the physical access layer for every downstream satellite deployment

companies.name: Rocket Lab USA
companies.notes: The only US rocket company besides SpaceX launching commercially on a regular cadence — serves small satellite operators for whom SpaceX's Falcon 9 is overkill, and is the US government's mandated second-source for national security launches.

tiers.fn: Design and produce the structural, power, thermal, propulsion, and attitude-control subsystems that host a payload in orbit

companies.name: Rocket Lab USA
companies.notes: Rocket Lab's satellite manufacturing arm builds the Photon and Pioneer spacecraft buses — giving it a vertically integrated space platform that captures revenue from both the launch and the spacecraft, unlike pure-launch rivals.

tiers.fn: Design and produce the mission-specific RF, optical, or sensing payload that transmits, receives, or collects data
Receive, process, and route satellite signals to and from end users; manage orbital slot, frequency, and antenna resources
Sell broadband, IoT, and narrowband connectivity services to end markets over owned or leased satellite capacity

companies.name: AST SpaceMobile
companies.notes: Building the first satellite network that connects directly to ordinary smartphones without any special hardware — backed by AT&T, Verizon, and Vodafone, who gain satellite coverage for every existing subscriber if the constellation deploys on schedule.

tiers.fn: Produce the antennas, modems, and enclosures that the end user installs to connect to the satellite network
Supply the radiation-hardened electronics, specialty materials, and environmental test services that enable satellite reliability in the space radiation environment
Coordinate, file, and manage radio frequency spectrum and orbital slot assignments across the full satellite lifecycle

tam.ai_driver: LEO mega-constellations (Starlink, Amazon Kuiper) provide the global backhaul for AI inference at the edge, while nascent on-orbit computing platforms are emerging as the next AI deployment frontier — satellite broadband expected to represent 50–70% of all space economy growth through 2040.

sectors.name: Compute Infrastructure
sectors.description: GPU compute cluster ready for AI training workload

tiers.fn: Design and assemble the server trays, HGX/MGX baseplates, and GPU compute nodes that house the accelerators

companies.name: Super Micro Computer
companies.notes: NVIDIA's preferred GPU server rack assembler — ships new GPU generations to hyperscalers faster than Dell or HPE, which is why customers who can't wait come here first, even at a premium.

companies.name: Wiwynn Corporation
companies.notes: Microsoft's primary ODM server partner — designs and assembles the AI server racks deployed in Azure datacenters. Benefits directly from Azure's accelerating GPU cluster deployments without technology risk beyond manufacturing execution.

companies.name: Penguin Solutions
companies.notes: Full-stack AI infrastructure integrator — bundles software-managed GPU clusters, specialized memory, and expert services into AI factory deployments. Captures margin across the entire AI infrastructure stack for enterprises that can't build internal AI infrastructure teams.

tiers.fn: Design, integrate, and operate the switches, NICs, and fabric interconnects that move data between GPUs, between servers, and between racks at AI-cluster scale

companies.name: Arista Networks
companies.notes: Dominates the high-speed Ethernet switches inside hyperscaler datacenters — its EOS operating system runs identically on every Arista switch ever made, locking customers into an automation layer they've spent years building and won't abandon for Cisco.

tiers.fn: Design, integrate, and operate the flash and object storage systems that hold AI training datasets, model checkpoints, and inference artifacts

companies.name: Pure Storage
companies.notes: The only major storage vendor that has never sold spinning hard drives — its all-flash-from-day-one architecture delivers simpler operations and faster data access than hybrid-storage competitors, increasingly critical for AI training datasets.

tiers.fn: Deliver, transform, condition, and distribute electrical power from utility feed to server board-level power rails, with protection and backup at each stage

companies.name: Vertiv Holdings
companies.notes: Supplies the power distribution and backup systems for AI datacenters — uniquely positioned for the industry shift to 800VDC direct current, which cuts conversion losses in dense GPU clusters and is being standardized across every major hyperscaler.

companies.name: Delta Electronics
companies.notes: Taiwan's leading power supply manufacturer — makes the server PSUs, UPS systems, and EV charging infrastructure that datacenters depend on. Delta PSUs are inside most major AI server brands including Super Micro and HPE, making it a quiet but essential supplier.

tiers.fn: Remove heat from compute nodes and reject it from the facility using the minimum energy input consistent with the server’s thermal design

companies.name: Vertiv Holdings
companies.notes: Makes the liquid cooling systems that high-density GPU racks require — its coolant distribution units (CDUs) are essential for AI chips that generate too much heat for air cooling to handle.

companies.name: Modine Manufacturing
companies.notes: Data center thermal management company pivoting from automotive cooling — its Climate Solutions segment makes precision liquid cooling for AI server racks, competing directly with Vertiv. The shift from air to liquid cooling in GPU-dense AI clusters is its core growth catalyst.

companies.name: Daikin Industries
companies.notes: The world's #1 HVAC company, expanding into data center precision cooling — its industrial chillers are critical for cooling the facilities housing AI GPU clusters. An indirect but large-scale beneficiary of the global AI infrastructure build-out.

companies.name: AVC (Delta subsidiary)
companies.notes: Taiwanese manufacturer of high-reliability cooling fans used inside servers and telecom equipment — every AI server rack contains dozens of these fans, making AVC a quiet structural beneficiary of the datacenter build-out.

companies.name: Auras Technology
companies.notes: Taiwanese thermal solutions maker designing heat sinks, vapor chambers, and thermal interface materials for high-density server applications — direct-liquid-cooling modules for AI GPU racks are its fastest-growing product line.

companies.name: nVent Electric
companies.notes: Makes thermal management enclosures and power protection systems for datacenters and industrial facilities — its Thermal Management segment directly serves high-density AI server rack deployments. A quieter Vertiv competitor with more diversified end-market exposure.

tiers.fn: Construct and operate the physical plant: structure, electrical infrastructure, mechanical systems, network entry, and physical security that define a data center tier
Operate the data center as a service platform: provision capacity, manage workloads, sell compute/storage/network as a service, and maintain SLA commitments

companies.name: IREN Limited
companies.notes: A former Bitcoin miner converting its cheap-power sites into AI compute rental — backed by anchor contracts with NVIDIA and Microsoft, with a permitted power pipeline that no non-hyperscaler GPU operator can match.

companies.name: Amazon (AWS)
companies.notes: AWS is the #1 cloud platform by revenue, powering most enterprise AI deployments — also developing custom silicon (Trainium for training, Inferentia for inference) to cut NVIDIA dependence. Cloud distribution plus in-house AI silicon gives AWS unique long-run unit economics.

companies.name: Alphabet (GCP)
companies.notes: GCP is the AI-native cloud with the deepest TPU integration — Google trains Gemini on its own hardware, giving it AI model and infrastructure advantages no other cloud replicates at the same depth. Also the most advanced in custom AI silicon design.

companies.name: Microsoft (Azure)
companies.notes: Azure is NVIDIA's largest single customer — its OpenAI partnership and Copilot integration make it the enterprise AI deployment platform of choice. The only hyperscaler with a locked-in AI application distribution advantage via Microsoft 365.

companies.name: Meta Platforms
companies.notes: Runs the world's largest private AI compute cluster outside China — building its own AI inference servers (Grand Teton) and custom silicon (MTIA) to serve 3.3B daily users without external cloud costs.

companies.name: Oracle Cloud
companies.notes: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) is the fastest-growing hyperscaler by percentage — its bare-metal GPU cluster model is preferred by AI labs wanting raw compute without cloud overhead. Increasingly the neutral cloud that AWS/Azure customers use to avoid vendor lock-in.

tam.ai_driver: Generative AI training and inference workloads are driving hyperscalers and GPU neoclouds to commit $260B+ in annual capex, with McKinsey forecasting AI-ready data center capacity growing at 33% CAGR through 2030.

sectors.name: Energy & Power
sectors.description: firm 24/7 clean power delivered to AI data center

tiers.fn: Convert a primary energy source (nuclear fission, combustion, photovoltaics, wind) into three-phase AC electricity at transmission voltage

companies.name: GE Vernova
companies.notes: The largest Western maker of gas turbines, wind turbines, and high-voltage grid equipment — its Grid Solutions division makes the transformers and switchgear connecting generation to AI datacenter loads. The only company at scale in both power generation and grid transmission hardware.

companies.name: Vistra Energy
companies.notes: The largest US independent power producer by generation capacity, with the country's largest nuclear fleet — its nuclear plants provide 24/7 firm power to a grid increasingly burdened by AI datacenter demand. Direct beneficiary of nuclear power's AI-driven renaissance.

companies.name: Mitsubishi Heavy Industries
companies.notes: Japan's largest industrial company and a key global gas turbine maker — its GTCC (gas turbine combined cycle) plants are among the most efficient gas power systems, serving AI-hungry economies across Asia and the Middle East.

tiers.fn: Transform, transmit, and switch electrical power at EHV/HV levels (66kV–800kV) from generation source to load center

companies.name: Siemens Energy
companies.notes: Makes the high-voltage transformers, switchgear, and grid technology that connects power plants to load centers — the backbone of every AI datacenter power delivery chain. Its HVDC technology is the preferred method for transmitting power from distant renewables to urban datacenter clusters.

tiers.fn: Store bulk electrical energy in electrochemical form and discharge on demand to provide capacity, frequency response, and arbitrage

companies.name: Fluence Energy
companies.notes: Builds and operates utility-scale battery storage systems — as renewable capacity grows faster than dispatchable generation, grid-scale batteries become the mechanism that firms up power supply for AI datacenter loads. Fluence is the #2 BESS integrator globally.

tiers.fn: Convert electrical power between AC and DC and across voltage levels at high efficiency using semiconductor switching devices

companies.name: Vertiv Holdings
companies.notes: Supplies the HVDC power conversion modules that feed AI datacenters directly from high-voltage bus — converts the AC power from the substation to the DC rails servers run on, reducing losses in the most power-hungry facilities ever built.

tiers.fn: Generate electricity from an electrochemical reaction (H2 + O2 or reformed natural gas) at the point of use, with high efficiency and low/zero emissions

companies.name: Bloom Energy
companies.notes: Sells solid oxide fuel cell systems that generate clean electricity on-site without grid dependency — its biogas and hydrogen-ready technology is being piloted at datacenters as firm-power alternative to the grid. The thesis is that AI facilities increasingly need distributed generation alongside grid power.

tiers.fn: Design, license, manufacture, and operate small modular reactors and microreactors that provide firm, dispatchable, carbon-free power at 1–500 MWe scale

companies.name: Oklo Inc.
companies.notes: Developing a 1.5 MW nuclear microreactor that runs on recycled nuclear waste fuel — targeting remote datacenters and military installations that need firm, carbon-free power with no dependency on grid infrastructure.

companies.name: T1 Energy Inc.
companies.notes: Provides construction and services for nuclear facilities — earns revenue from the nuclear buildout regardless of whether individual reactor operators succeed, a lower-risk position than owning the reactors.

tiers.fn: Plan, dispatch, monitor, and optimize the flow of electricity across generation assets, transmission networks, and end-use loads using software and communications infrastructure

tam.ai_driver: AI servers consume 5–10× more power per rack than traditional servers — IEA projects global data center electricity demand will more than double to ~945 TWh by 2030, requiring massive grid upgrades and accelerating adoption of GaN/SiC power semiconductors and on-site clean energy.
tam.ai_sub_label: Data center power supply market 2030 (Yole Group)

tiers.fn: Provides silicon-level root of trust — the cryptographic anchor that all higher software tiers depend on for attestation and key storage
Governs who and what can authenticate and authorize access to systems, data, and cloud resources — first control plane

companies.name: SailPoint, Inc.
companies.notes: Governs who can access what across large enterprises — enforces least-privilege access for thousands of employees and IT systems, shrinking the attack surface before any breach occurs. ~120% net revenue retention signals strong lock-in.

tiers.fn: Monitors every process, file, registry, and network call on endpoints to detect and contain threats in real time

companies.name: CrowdStrike
companies.notes: The definitive AI-native endpoint security platform — Falcon runs on 28M+ endpoints and detects novel threats without signature updates. Its Charlotte AI security agent sets the autonomous threat detection standard that every EDR competitor benchmarks against.

tiers.fn: Inspects east-west and north-south traffic flows to detect anomalies, lateral movement, and command-and-control communications

companies.name: Fortinet
companies.notes: Network security appliance leader with a price-performance advantage over Palo Alto Networks for enterprise and mid-market — FortiGate firewalls dominate network perimeters, and its unified platform bundles SD-WAN, firewall, and NDR at a cost structure competitors struggle to match.

tiers.fn: Continuously audits cloud infrastructure configurations and workload runtime behavior against security benchmarks and compliance frameworks
Protects data at rest and in motion through encryption, DLP, and immutable backup — and recovers from ransomware or deletion

companies.name: Rubrik, Inc.
companies.notes: Specializes in ransomware-proof backups — its immutable snapshot architecture ensures attackers can't delete recovery copies, directly solving the most common way ransomware gangs extort victims. Cloud-native vs. legacy Veeam and Commvault.

tiers.fn: Aggregates, correlates, and responds to telemetry from all other tiers — the analytical hub of the security stack

companies.name: Palo Alto Networks
companies.notes: Platform security company consolidating SIEM, CSPM, EDR, and firewall under one AI-powered subscription — Cortex XDR and Prisma Cloud dominate enterprise security operations. Its platformization strategy converts point-solution spending into recurring revenue, driving margin expansion as customers consolidate vendors.

tiers.fn: Secures AI/ML systems themselves and deploys AI agents for autonomous threat hunting — the emerging frontier tier

tam.ai_driver: AI-powered attacks (automated phishing, deepfakes, autonomous malware) are forcing enterprises to deploy AI-native defenses — the AI-in-cybersecurity sub-segment alone is projected to grow at 24.4% CAGR from $25B to $94B by 2030.
tam.ai_sub_label: AI in cybersecurity market 2030 (Grand View Research)

sectors.name: AI Model Infrastructure
sectors.description: Foundation model training, optimization, and serving

tiers.fn: Acquire, clean, deduplicate, and prepare large-scale datasets for model pre-training, RLHF, and fine-tuning
Orchestrate GPU/TPU clusters at scale for model pre-training and reinforcement learning using parallelism strategies that saturate interconnect bandwidth

companies.name: CoreWeave
companies.notes: Pure-play GPU cloud operator and NVIDIA's preferred hyperscale partner — provides the bare-metal H100/B200 clusters that AI labs use for frontier model training when they can't get time on AWS or Azure. Its 2025 IPO made it the first listed company whose entire business is GPU-as-a-service.

tiers.fn: Design neural architectures, execute pre-training runs, and establish base model capabilities across language, vision, and multimodal tasks
Adapt foundation models to be helpful, harmless, and honest via supervised fine-tuning, preference learning, and safety evaluation
Reduce model size, latency, and compute cost for production inference without unacceptable quality loss
Serve model inference at scale with low P50/P95 latency, high throughput, and cost-efficient GPU utilization using continuous batching, KV cache management, and speculative decoding
Manage the end-to-end model lifecycle: experiment tracking, versioning, CI/CD pipelines, feature management, drift monitoring, and automated retraining

companies.name: Datadog
companies.notes: Monitoring platform expanding into LLM Observability — tracks token costs, latency, error rates, and prompt quality across AI model deployments. The default APM for AI-native applications, adding an LLM-specific revenue layer on top of its infrastructure monitoring base.

companies.name: Snowflake
companies.notes: Data platform adding Cortex AI — brings managed LLM inference, vector search, and a feature store directly into the data warehouse where training data lives. Removes the need to move data to a separate ML platform, compressing the RAG and fine-tuning workflow into one SQL environment.

companies.name: Palantir Technologies
companies.notes: Enterprise AI deployment platform with US government as a marquee anchor — its AIP (AI Platform) orchestrates LLM agents over proprietary enterprise data using its Ontology layer, solving the governance and data-access problems that prevent most enterprises from deploying AI safely.

companies.name: MongoDB
companies.notes: Document database adding native vector search (HNSW) for RAG pipelines — enables developers to store embeddings alongside operational data without a separate vector store. Positioned as the simplest path from structured application data to LLM retrieval-augmented generation.

tiers.fn: Expose model capabilities via standardized APIs, SDKs, and developer tooling consumed by application builders and enterprise integrators

companies.name: Confluent
companies.notes: Kafka-based real-time streaming platform for ML feature pipelines — streams transactional events to model serving endpoints in milliseconds, enabling real-time fraud scoring, recommendation serving, and feature freshness that batch ETL cannot match.

tam.source: IDC AI & GenAI Spending Guide 2024 / MarketsandMarkets 2024
tam.ai_driver: Enterprise deployment of LLMs is shifting spend from one-time training compute toward always-on inference infrastructure, API platforms, and MLOps tooling — IDC projects AI/GenAI software and services spending reaching $202B by 2028 at a 59% CAGR.

sectors.name: Robotics & Edge AI
sectors.description: Embodied AI systems — autonomous robots, edge inference, perception hardware

tiers.fn: Provides multi-modal sensing of the physical environment (geometry, semantics, identity)

companies.name: Ouster, Inc.
companies.notes: Makes digital LiDAR sensors for autonomous robots, industrial automation, and smart infrastructure — its custom SoC architecture is more cost-scalable than analog LiDAR, with gross margins expanding from 22% to 45%. Pentagon Blue UAS approval opens defense channels.

tiers.fn: Runs real-time perception, planning, and control inference at the robot or device

companies.name: Ambarella, Inc.
companies.notes: Designs the AI vision SoCs that run real-time perception inference inside security cameras, autonomous vehicles, and robots — its CVflow architecture delivers GPU-class AI at <20W, eliminating the need to send video to the cloud for analysis. The default edge AI chip for computer vision at scale.

tiers.fn: Converts electrical commands into precise physical motion
Provides the software framework for sensor-actuator integration, inter-node communication, and system orchestration
Trains perception, planning, and language-conditioned task models; deploys quantized versions to edge
Integrates subsystems into a deployable robot or edge device and validates end-to-end system behavior
Monitors and manages deployed robot fleets across sites in real time

tam.ai_driver: Foundation models adapted into world models for physical environments are collapsing the cost and programming effort for industrial robots and humanoids — Goldman Sachs projects humanoids alone at $38B by 2035, with AI-powered robots growing at 40% CAGR through 2030.

sectors.name: Fintech & Commerce AI
sectors.description: AI-powered financial services and commerce platforms

tiers.fn: Provides the clearing and settlement backbone for all money movement
Maintains authoritative books of record for accounts, balances, and transactions
Detects and suppresses fraudulent transactions and credit risk events in real time
Establishes and maintains verified identity for onboarding and ongoing compliance
Scores loan applications and prices risk using alternative and traditional data sources

companies.name: SoFi Technologies
companies.notes: The only fintech with a national bank charter, a consumer financial platform, AND a B2B payment infrastructure (Galileo, serving 158M accounts) — three compounding layers no other digital bank has combined.

tiers.fn: Delivers end-user financial management, investment, and advisory experiences

companies.name: Robinhood Markets
companies.notes: Made stock trading free and built a large young user base — now expanding into wealth management and crypto to deepen the financial relationship beyond zero-commission trades, though it lacks SoFi's bank charter and B2B moat.

tiers.fn: Delivers banking and payment capabilities inside non-financial applications via API

companies.name: MercadoLibre
companies.notes: The only two-sided compounder in Latin America — Mercado Libre (e-commerce) and MercadoPago (fintech) share the same customer base across 18 countries, with Mercado Credito lending to customers who lack access to traditional banking.

tiers.fn: Automates compliance reporting, transaction monitoring, and regulatory filing obligations

companies.name: Walmart
companies.notes: The world's largest retailer building an embedded financial services layer — leveraging its 200M+ weekly customer base to offer banking, payments, and credit to underserved consumers via One Finance and Walmart Pay. Fintech revenue is nascent but the distribution advantage is unmatched by any pure-play competitor.

tam.source: MarketsandMarkets AI in Finance 2024 / Morgan Stanley 2025
tam.ai_driver: Generative AI is compressing the cost of financial decisioning (fraud, credit, trading) while agentic shopping agents are emerging as a new distribution layer that intercepts purchase intent before it reaches any traditional storefront — Morgan Stanley estimates AI agents will influence $385B in U.S. e-commerce spend by 2030.

edges.product: SiC MOSFET / GaN HEMT → inverter and rectifier modules
GPU / CPU / ASIC → compute node PCB
NdFeB permanent magnet → wind turbine PMSG
Nvidia Jetson Orin / Ambarella CV3 → edge inference module
GPU compute hours (H100/B200 DGX clusters) → model pre-training runs
GPU die (H100/B200) + packaged HBM → training accelerator module

tech_races.name: NAND vs. HBM4
tech_races.preference: NAND over HBM4 for the agentic AI demand wave
tech_races.application_driver: Agentic AI requires large persistent stores (RAG, vector DB, session state), high-frequency sequential reads, non-volatile persistence across power cycles, and edge deployment for autonomous agents — all served by NAND. HBM4 serves on-package bandwidth for training and frontier inference: a different and already-priced-in demand signal.
consensus_gap: Memory = HBM = the AI memory play
HBM is GPU-tied and already recognized. NAND's agentic AI TAM — enterprise SSDs, edge agents, NVMe tiering — is underappreciated.
NAND is commodity with limited moat
Leading-edge QLC and enterprise NVMe have meaningful process differentiation. SanDisk/KIOXIA at cutting-edge node.
HBM4 is the next big upgrade cycle
HBM4 serves incremental GPU bandwidth. NAND serves horizontal scale-out of deployed agentic infrastructure.
bull_case: Enterprise SSD attach per agentic deployment grows from near-zero to standard infrastructure
Automotive NAND (in-vehicle agents, OTA model updates) is underpenetrated and accelerating
QLC NAND cost-per-bit reaches crossover displacing hard drives in warm storage tier
NAND pricing recovers from 2023–2024 oversupply trough; supply discipline restored
bear_risk: Chinese NAND producers (YMTC) gain market access and compress ASPs
Compute-in-memory architectures reduce the NAND read bottleneck long-term
Agentic workloads shift toward stateless designs, reducing persistent storage demand

tech_races.name: Compute Architecture Bifurcation
tech_races.preference: CPU (orchestration) + Custom ASIC (training/inference) — GPU dominant but bifurcating

tickers.weighting: Underweight vs. ARM on architecture preference

tech_races.application_driver: Agentic AI has three distinct compute layers: orchestration (tool dispatch, routing, memory management — CPU-bound), frontier inference (large LLMs — GPU/custom ASIC), and edge inference (vehicles, robots, laptops — NPU/custom ASIC, power-constrained). The pure-GPU narrative oversimplifies this bifurcation. Every GPU server still needs CPUs to orchestrate; edge agents can't run GPUs.
consensus_gap: CPU orchestration layers mean high-core-count CPUs remain essential — they're the coordination fabric around GPU clusters.
Custom ASICs (TPU, Trainium, MTIA) are capturing training share at hyperscalers. AVGO and MRVL are the beneficiaries.
ARM is just a mobile chip story
ARM's NPU IP is the critical differentiator for edge inference. Server market share (Graviton, Neoverse) is growing fast.
bull_case: Hyperscaler custom ASIC capex grows from ~10% to ~30% of silicon budget over 3 years
ARM server CPU reaches 25% of data center sockets by 2028 (vs. ~10% today)
Edge AI inference (automotive, robotics) becomes the next 100M unit market won by ARM NPU
bear_risk: CUDA moat proves stronger than expected; custom ASICs remain sub-scale and high-NRE
AMD catches NVIDIA before ARM disrupts CPUs — pure GPU narrative extends
RISC-V open-source pressure erodes ARM's edge licensing power

tech_races.name: Optical Interconnect
tech_races.preference: CPO (co-packaged optics) is the structural winner — pluggable extends 2–3 more years

tickers.weighting: Overweight on pluggable cycle; watch CPO readiness
Market weight; near-term revenue + CPO optionality
Market weight; CPO is a structural headwind
High upside if CPO ramps; binary risk on timing
No weighting change — agnostic to pluggable vs. CPO

tech_races.application_driver: AI cluster networking at 1.6T+ per port hits the power wall for pluggable transceivers — the electrical trace from switch ASIC to pluggable cage burns too much power. CPO eliminates that trace by integrating the optical engine directly on or beside the switch ASIC. Every major switch ASIC roadmap (Broadcom, Marvell, Intel) assumes CPO at 3.2T+.
consensus_gap: Buy pluggable transceiver makers as the AI networking play
Pluggable wins now (400G/800G), but CPO is the structural endpoint at 3.2T+. The question is timing, not direction.
CPO is too complex to commercialize
TSMC N45SiPh and Intel IFS are production SiPh PDKs. Hyperscalers are actively co-developing CPO solutions for 2026–2027.
POET is too early to matter
CPO-first platforms have the right architecture. Risk is timing, not concept viability.
bull_case: 1.6T pluggable proves too power-hungry at scale; hyperscalers pull CPO forward to 2026
POET or competing CPO platform wins a major switch ASIC design-in at revenue scale
CPO eliminates the QSFP-DD connector tier ahead of schedule — incumbent module makers face structural disruption
bear_risk: 1.6T pluggable works well enough; CPO pushed to 2028+ giving pluggable more runway
CPO integration complexity (foundry + photonics alignment) causes repeated delays
CPO solution is captive/in-house (NVIDIA, Intel) — investable pure plays don't capture the win
